mnemonics.title: STEGO = COVER
mnemonics.content: Steganography comes from Greek 'steganos' (covered) + 'graphein' (writing) = COVERED WRITING. The message is covered/hidden inside another medium, not scrambled like cryptography.

mnemonics.title: Crypto vs Stego
mnemonics.content: Cryptography = Content Protection (makes message unreadable). Steganography = Concealment Practice (makes message invisible). Crypto changes WHAT you see; Stego changes WHETHER you see it.

mnemonics.title: LSB = Last bit, Smallest change
mnemonics.content: Least Significant Bit: the LAST bit of each pixel byte. Changing it shifts the pixel value by only 1 (e.g., 200 to 201). Smallest possible change = hardest to detect visually.

mnemonics.title: ICR = Three Requirements
mnemonics.content: Good steganography needs: Imperceptibility (can not see the hidden data), Capacity (enough room for the message), Robustness (survives compression/editing). ICR = I Can not Really see it.

mnemonics.title: Steganalysis = Stego + Analysis
mnemonics.content: Steganalysis detects hidden messages. Methods: Statistical analysis (pixel distribution anomalies), Visual inspection, Comparison with clean originals, File structure analysis. The art of finding what steganography hides.

keyPoints: Steganography hides the EXISTENCE of a message, unlike cryptography which hides the CONTENT.
LSB (Least Significant Bit) replacement is the most common image steganography technique.
Carrier media include images, audio, video, and text files.
There is a fundamental trade-off between embedding capacity and imperceptibility.
Combining steganography with cryptography provides defense in depth (hiding existence + protecting content).
Steganalysis is the science of detecting hidden steganographic messages.
commonMistakes: Confusing steganography (hiding existence) with cryptography (hiding content).
Assuming steganography alone provides sufficient security; if the hidden message is found, it is readable unless also encrypted.
Thinking LSB steganography is undetectable; statistical steganalysis can detect the altered bit distributions.
Forgetting the capacity-imperceptibility trade-off: embedding too much data makes detection easier.
